title: Show the Real User Cron Jobs
hook: Cron problems often hide behind comments, blank lines, and copied folklore.
problem: You need to inspect a user's scheduled cron commands without editing the crontab.
starting_state: A user may have scheduled jobs, environment lines, comments, and blank lines in their crontab.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints the active, non-comment crontab lines.
when_to_use: Use when a job ran unexpectedly or did not run and you need the user's cron schedule first.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as a full system scheduler audit; cron.d, cron.daily, and systemd timers live elsewhere.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Environment assignments and active cron schedule lines.

shorts.title: See active cron lines only.
shorts.voiceover: Before editing cron, strip comments and blanks. Now you can see which jobs actually exist for this user.
shorts.caption: Cron triage starts read-only.

linkedin.question: When a cron job misbehaves, do you check the user crontab or system cron first?
linkedin.cta: Save this as the first cron triage step.

youtube.description: Show active user crontab entries without editing anything.

experiments.variant_a: Stop opening cron in edit mode first.
experiments.variant_b: Show active cron jobs without touching them.

title: Find System Cron Files Fast
hook: A job can be nowhere in your crontab and still run every night.
problem: System cron jobs can live in /etc/cron.d and periodic directories, so checking only crontab -l misses them.
starting_state: The server has system cron directories with package-managed and app-managed job files.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command lists system cron files in stable order.
when_to_use: Use when a scheduled task runs as root, a service user, or a package job rather than the current user.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume every listed file is executable or valid under run-parts naming rules.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Sorted paths under /etc/cron.d and periodic cron directories.

shorts.title: Cron is bigger than crontab.
shorts.voiceover: If the user crontab is empty, do not stop. System cron files can still launch backups, cleanup, and package jobs.
shorts.caption: Check /etc/cron.* too.

linkedin.question: How often do you find production jobs hiding outside user crontabs?
linkedin.cta: Add this to your scheduler audit checklist.

youtube.description: Find system cron files across cron.d and periodic cron directories.

experiments.variant_a: The job was not in crontab -l.
experiments.variant_b: Cron has more hiding places than people remember.

title: Turn Cron Into a Readable Table
hook: Cron is easier to debug when the schedule and command stop blending together.
problem: Raw crontab lines are hard to scan during an incident, especially with long commands.
starting_state: A user crontab contains active schedule lines and command strings.
what_changed: Nothing changes. awk formats the five schedule fields separately from the command.
when_to_use: Use when reviewing several cron jobs and you need a quick schedule-to-command map.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it for /etc/cron.d files without adapting for the user field after the schedule.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Aligned schedule expressions next to their command strings.

shorts.title: Make cron readable.
shorts.voiceover: Cron fields are compact until you are tired. Split the schedule from the command and the review gets faster.
shorts.caption: Readable cron beats guessing.

linkedin.question: Do you mentally parse cron fields, or do you format them before reviewing?
linkedin.cta: Use this for fast cron reviews.

youtube.description: Format a user crontab into a readable schedule and command table.

experiments.variant_a: Cron is dense. Format it first.
experiments.variant_b: Make cron readable before debugging it.

title: Find Cron Jobs With No Log Trail
hook: A silent cron job is a future incident with no witness.
problem: Cron jobs without redirects, mail, or logger calls can fail without leaving an obvious trail.
starting_state: A user crontab has a mix of logged and silent scheduled commands.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints active lines that do not appear to capture output.
when_to_use: Use before relying on cron for backups, reports, billing, imports, or cleanup.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat this as proof the job is broken; it is a prompt to inspect observability.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Cron lines that lack obvious logging or notification patterns.

shorts.title: Find silent cron jobs.
shorts.voiceover: If cron runs a backup with no redirect and no mail, your first clue may be missing data. Flag silent jobs before they matter.
shorts.caption: No log trail, no confidence.

linkedin.question: What is your minimum logging standard for cron jobs that touch production data?
linkedin.cta: Use this as a cron observability audit.

youtube.description: Find cron jobs that appear to lack logging or notification.

experiments.variant_a: Silent cron jobs are future incidents.
experiments.variant_b: Find cron jobs with no log trail.

title: Catch Cron Daily Files That Will Be Skipped
hook: A dot in a filename can keep a cron.daily script from running.
problem: run-parts only executes files matching its naming rules, so backup scripts with dots can be ignored.
starting_state: Periodic cron directories contain executable-looking files with mixed naming styles.
what_changed: Nothing changes. run-parts prints which files it would run.
when_to_use: Use when a script in cron.daily, cron.hourly, cron.weekly, or cron.monthly does not appear to execute.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to run the jobs; --test is only a dry inspection.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: A list of periodic cron scripts that pass run-parts selection rules.

shorts.title: Why cron.daily skipped it.
shorts.voiceover: The file can exist, be executable, and still be skipped. Test what run-parts would actually run.
shorts.caption: run-parts --test is your witness.

linkedin.question: Have you ever lost time to a cron.daily filename that run-parts ignored?

youtube.description: Use run-parts --test to inspect which periodic cron scripts would run.

experiments.variant_a: A dot can break your cron.daily plan.
experiments.variant_b: The script existed. Cron still skipped it.

title: Map systemd Timers to Services
hook: A timer is only half the scheduled job. The service is the payload.
problem: systemctl list-timers shows timer units, but you need to see what service each timer activates.
starting_state: The server uses systemd timers for backups, certificate renewal, log rotation, or reports.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command condenses timer rows into timer-to-service mappings.
when_to_use: Use when auditing scheduled work on modern Linux systems that may not rely only on cron.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to inspect the service definition itself; follow up with systemctl cat on the unit.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.

shorts.title: Timers are not the job.
shorts.voiceover: The timer decides when. The service decides what. Map them before you debug the wrong unit.
shorts.caption: systemd timer -> service.

linkedin.question: When auditing scheduled work, do you map timers to services explicitly?
linkedin.cta: Add timer activation mapping to your systemd checklist.

youtube.description: Map systemd timer units to the services they activate.

experiments.variant_a: The timer is not the payload.

title: Spot Stale systemd Timers
hook: The suspicious timer is the one with no next run.
problem: A timer may exist but have no next scheduled run, which can hide disabled or stale automation.
starting_state: Timer output includes active timers and at least one stale timer with no upcoming trigger.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command filters the timer list for stale-looking and important automation rows.
when_to_use: Use when backups, reports, renewals, or cleanup jobs stopped running after a migration or package change.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume n/a is always bad; some timers are intentionally inactive until enabled or triggered.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Timer rows including n/a NEXT values and important scheduled jobs.

shorts.title: Find stale timers.
shorts.voiceover: If a timer has no next run, it may be disabled, stale, or intentionally parked. Either way, it deserves a look.
shorts.caption: No NEXT run? Investigate.

linkedin.question: Which timers on your servers would hurt if they silently stopped running?
linkedin.cta: Use this for scheduled-job drift checks.

youtube.description: Filter systemd timers for stale-looking rows and critical scheduled automation.

experiments.variant_a: The suspicious timer has no next run.
experiments.variant_b: Find stale automation before it finds you.

title: Check the Last Timer Payload Logs
hook: When a timer fires, the useful logs are usually on the service.
problem: A systemd timer appears scheduled, but you need the recent output from the service it activates.
starting_state: A systemd timer activates a service such as backup.service, and you need recent service logs.
what_changed: Nothing changes. journalctl prints the latest lines for the service unit.
when_to_use: Use after mapping a timer to its service and confirming the service name.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as proof the next timer will run; pair it with list-timers for schedule state.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Recent service log lines showing start, completion, warnings, or errors.

shorts.title: Read the payload logs.
shorts.voiceover: The timer tells you when it fired. The service logs tell you what happened after it fired.
shorts.caption: Timer triage ends in journalctl.

linkedin.question: Do you inspect timer units first or the activated service logs first?
linkedin.cta: Use this pair for systemd timer incidents.

youtube.description: Use journalctl on the service activated by a systemd timer to inspect the last run.

experiments.variant_a: The useful logs are on the service.

title: Dry-Run Logrotate Before Touching Logs
hook: Logrotate can explain its plan without rotating anything.
problem: You need to understand what logrotate would do without compressing, renaming, or truncating real logs.
starting_state: The server has logrotate config files and logs that may or may not need rotation.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The -d flag runs logrotate in debug mode and the pipe extracts useful decision lines.
when_to_use: Use before changing logrotate rules or when logs are growing and you need to see whether policies match them.
when_not_to_use: Do not use -f unless you intentionally want to force rotation; this lesson is debug-only.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only in debug mode.
expected_output: rotating pattern and considering log lines from logrotate debug output.

shorts.title: Dry-run logrotate first.
shorts.voiceover: Do not force log rotation to see what will happen. Debug mode explains the matching rules without touching the logs.
shorts.caption: logrotate -d is read-only triage.

linkedin.question: Do you dry-run logrotate before changing rotation rules?
linkedin.cta: Save this for disk and log-growth incidents.

youtube.description: Use logrotate debug mode to inspect rotation decisions without modifying log files.

experiments.variant_a: Logrotate can explain its plan first.

title: Find Logs Missing Logrotate Coverage
hook: The biggest log risk is often the file no policy mentions.
problem: A log file can grow forever if no logrotate rule references it.
command: find /var/log -type f -name '*.log' -printf '%p\n' | while read -r log; do grep -Rqs -- "$log" /etc/logrotate.conf /etc/logrotate.d || grep -Rqs -- "$(dirname "$log")/[*].log" /etc/logrotate.conf /etc/logrotate.d || printf '%s\n' "$log"; done
starting_state: The server has several .log files and logrotate rules covering only some of them.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The loop prints log files that are not directly referenced by logrotate config text.
when_to_use: Use during disk-growth triage or before onboarding a new app that writes its own logs.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat absence from this output as complete proof of coverage; wildcard patterns can require manual review.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Paths to .log files that do not appear directly in logrotate configuration.

commands: find /var/log -type f -name '*.log' -printf '%p\n' | while read -r log; do grep -Rqs -- "$log" /etc/logrotate.conf /etc/logrotate.d || grep -Rqs -- "$(dirname "$log")/[*].log" /etc/logrotate.conf /etc/logrotate.d || printf '%s\n' "$log"; done

shorts.title: Find unmanaged logs.
shorts.voiceover: If an app writes a log that logrotate never mentions, disk alerts are only a matter of time. List uncovered files first.
shorts.caption: Unmanaged logs become disk incidents.

linkedin.question: How do you check that every app log has a rotation policy?
linkedin.cta: Use this as a first pass before manual policy review.

youtube.description: Find .log files that are not directly referenced by logrotate config.

experiments.variant_a: The file no policy mentions is the risk.
